revision.content:
We've had bluebirds eating from our feeders ever since my wife and I moved into our house a few years ago. We put up bluebird houses and they took to them quickly, but we also found house sparrows which compete with bluebirds filling up our boxes too. I started planning out building a birdhouse with a camera in it in the fall of 2024 ahead of the coming spring. In early winter I ordered an outdoor wifi camera with the hopes of installing it in one of my existing bluebird houses. Many years ago I had put a web cam outside a pigeon nest and captured a season of baby birds hatching and growing and it was a lot of fun.

[[Image:Birdhouse_Cam.png|none|thumb|300px|Bluebird house with camera]]


== The parts ==
* An already seasoned cedar bluebird house.
* A cheap outdoor wifi camera
* Aluminum flashing
* Silicone sealer
* A solar panel with built-in battery (spoiler alert: this did not work out)
* [https://www.aliexpress.us/item/2255800296460053.html](later: a 12v AC power supply and ~30' of speaker cable)


=== Camera notes ===
From the link you can select cameras with different focal points, I chose the one with the shortest focal point (2.1mm) and this was perfect for this build. It should be possible to manually adjust the focal point but the less work one has to do the better of course.
* This is the camera I used - [https://www.aliexpress.us/item/2255800346161065.html]
* "1920P1080P 4MP Mini Waterproof IP66 TF Card Slot IR Night Vision IP Camera Wifi Outdoor Car & Vehicle Fleet & Bird Nest"

{|
| [[Image:Wifi_birdhouse_camera.png|none|thumb|200px|4MP camera]]
| [[Image:Birdhouse_cam_web_admin.png|none|thumb|250px|Camera web admin page]]
| [[Image:Birdhouse_cam_wifi.png|none|thumb|300px|WIFI config]]
|}


While the camera is capable of wifi, it also has a cable that breaks out to an RJ45 jack, a 12v (4W) input, and a reset button. The camera comes with directions to install a phone app for the setup which wants all permissions to do whatever Chinesium things it thinks it needs to do. That's not necessary if you can plug the ethernet into a network with DHCP. Once it's accessible via ethernet, you can connect to the admin page on the camera and setup it's wifi access to your network. Once on wifi you can ditch the hardline. The only real issue I've had with the camera so far is that it continually resets the date to 2023-11 even with NTP setup. I'm not particularly bothered by that, your mileage may vary.


== The build ==
Due to space constraints I had to cut a hole in the roof of the bird house for the camera. While the camera is rated for outdoor use, I've found that any extra protection from the elements you can provide will extend the life of an outdoor camera. I also added a piece of plexiglass to protect the camera from activity inside the birdhouse. This had a slight side-effect of reflecting the IR led's at night as you'll be able to see later. If you build a similar setup you can decide if it bothers you enough to do something different with your build.

{|
| [[Image:BirdhouseCam01.jpeg|none|thumb|200px|Camera mounted, some nails to route the camera cable]]
| [[Image:BirdhouseCam05.jpeg|none|thumb|200px|Camera with plexi shield]]
| [[Image:BirdhouseCam02.jpeg|none|thumb|200px|Birds-eye-view]]
| [[Image:BirdhouseCam04.jpeg|none|thumb|200px|Wider view]]
|}


== Mostly complete ==
Here you can see the flashing installed over the roof. There are actually three pieces of flashing, the main cover and a piece on both sides to keep rain from blowing in the sides where a gap was still required between the flashing and the wood for the camera power cable. I added some creases to the flashing as you can see. During the design phase I had entered into analysis paralysis on how to power the camera, to break the stale-mate I just yolo'd solar panel with built-in battery pack from Amazon without any actual considerations. It is capable of outputting the required 12v the camera needs, but I had no idea if it had the capacity to run the camera correctly.

One thing not in the photos is a dollop of silicone sealer I added around the wifi antenna. 

{|
| [[Image:BirdhouseCam06.jpeg|none|300px|...]]
| [[Image:BirdhouseCam07.jpeg|none|300px|...]]
|}


== Test run with solar panel ==
Once I finally had everything ready to mount on the bird house post I did a test run with the solar panel. The camera ran for about 1-2 hours off the solar panel battery with the panel sitting in full daylight. This was obviously not going to run the camera 24x7 so that was a no-go. I already suspected this to be the case, so my backup plan was to suspend some speaker wire from our outbuilding to the birdhouse to power the camera. I already had infrastructure in place for this for powering IR illuminators mounted under my soffits so this was not a terribly difficult switch-up. I used eye-screws on both ends to support the cable and zip-ties to secure the wire to the eye-screw and provide strain relief on each end. On the outbuilding I ran the wire through a hole already present on the underside of the soffit (with a grommet to protect the wires) up into the attic and connected it to a 12V AC adapter from there. 

{|
| [[Image:BirdhouseCam08.jpeg|none|200px|Bluebird house mounted]]
| [[Image:BirdhouseCam09.jpeg|none|200px|Power cable strain relief]]
| [[Image:BirdhouseCam010.jpeg|none|300px|Strain relief and soffit hole to access attic power]]
|}

In the middle picture you can see a mylar bag secured to the backside of the birdhouse with a black ziptie around the entire birdhouse. This contains the weather sensitive ends of the wifi camera cables. They are stuffed up inside the bag (RJ45 jack, power jack, and reset button). The power cable (speaker wire) runs up inside the bag and connects to the cameras power jack. I cut the 2.1mm power connector off of the AC adapter and spliced it onto the cable that connects to the camera.


== Birds start to check it out ==
The bluebirds immediately started getting into the birdhouse a few times a day which was awesome. After about three weeks they got more serious and started building a real nest (late March at this point). We've been told that bluebirds can take two weeks to 2 days to build a nest, the first nest they build each season seems to take longer to complete though. They can have multiple broods each year.
{|
| [[Image:Screen_Shot_2025-03-26_at_10.52.57_AM.png|none|300px|Bluebird inspection]]
| [[Image:Screen_Shot_2025-03-26_at_11.00.48_AM.png|none|300px|Bluebird]]
| [[Image:Screen_Shot_2025-03-27_at_11.53.35_AM.png|none|300px|Night-shot]]
|}


== Unexpected visitor ==
One night before the bluebirds had moved in I checked and found something unexpected: a tree frog! It stayed in the birdhouse over night. The next morning we saw the bluebird enter the house, see the frog, then leave. At that point I intervened and chased the frog out, it was about the size of my thumb. Afterwords the bluebirds continued building their nest in the box. After doing more research, apparently it's not uncommon for tree frogs to inhabit a birdhouse from time to time.
[[Image:Screen_Shot_2025-03-28_at_10.53.32_PM.png|none|400px|Unexpected visitor]]

The tree frog returned the following night and had to be chased out a second time. A suitable temp housing situation was created by planting a PVC pipe in the ground near the birdhouse and the frog gently lowered into it. This seems to have abated the frog squatting situation for now. However a grander wood frog house was acquired at a hobby store, stained, and polyurethaned.


== First Egg! ==
On April 6th after a regional monsoon gave us the worst flooding this area had had since 2008, our bluebird laid her first egg!
 
{|
| [[Image:Birdhouse_cam_first_egg.png|none|300px|First bluebird egg]]
| [[Image:Birdhouse_cam_bluebird01.png|none|300px|Bluebird]]
|}

I was pleased that the inside of the bluebird house stayed dry through three days of constant rain despite my modifications, apparently my flashing and silicone work was adequate. 

== They grow up fast! ==
Blue birds take from 16 to 24 days to go from hatched to leaving the nest!

{|
| [[Image:Birdhouse_cam_eggs-5.png|none|350px|And then there were five eggs]]
| [[Image:Birdhouse_cam_eggs-hatched01.png|none|350px|One day old: 4 of 5 eggs hatched]]
| [[Image:Birdhouse_cam_5_babies.png|none|350px|Five sleeping baby bluebirds]]
|}

{|
| [[Image:Birdhouse_cam_hungry_mouths.png|none|350px|Five hungry mouths]]
| [[Image:Birdhouse_cam_last_day.png|none|350px|The day before leaving the nest]]
| [[Image:Birdhouse_cam_empty_nest.png|none|350px|Empty nest]]
|}

== Brood 2 ==
After the first brood left the nest I cleared out the old nest. A week or two afterwords our momma bluebird built a second nest and laid another 5 eggs. Unfortunately, one morning we looked and mamma bird and the eggs were all gone. I did not have the camera saving photos anywhere so we don't know what got into the box, but it was a sad morning. I got my second camera birdhouse setup not long after and we did get another bluebird nest in there. From the other box, three of the five eggs hatched and were raised to the point they left on their own, so overall there was more success than failure.



[[Category:Dosman projects]]
